---
title: "Chapter 13 — 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 12 · 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning](ch12.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [14 · 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals →](ch14.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Probabilistic graphical models (PGMs) are a marriage of probability theory and graph theory: they use a graph to encode how a large collection of random variables depend on one another, so that a joint distribution which would otherwise be hopelessly large can be written, reasoned about, and learned. They matter because almost every interesting problem — diagnosing a patient, parsing a sentence, tracking an object — involves many uncertain quantities that interact, and PGMs give a principled language for exactly those interactions. They sit at the heart of probabilistic and statistical ML, and their ideas quietly resurface inside modern deep generative models.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Probabilistic & statistical ML · used to represent and reason about many interacting uncertain variables · the one key idea: a graph turns a giant joint distribution into a product of small local factors.
:::

## 13.1 — Why graphs: factorizing the joint distribution

Imagine you have $n$ binary variables. Their joint distribution $P(X_1, \dots, X_n)$ has $2^n - 1$ free numbers. At $n = 30$ that is already a billion. You cannot store it, estimate it from data, or compute anything with it. This is the curse that PGMs exist to break.

The escape is **conditional independence**. Two variables $A$ and $B$ are conditionally independent given $C$ — written $A \perp B \mid C$ — if, once you know $C$, learning $A$ tells you nothing more about $B$: $P(A, B \mid C) = P(A \mid C)\,P(B \mid C)$. Real systems are riddled with such structure. Whether your car starts depends on the battery and the fuel; given those, the weather last Tuesday is irrelevant.

A graphical model is just a picture of which independencies hold. Nodes are variables; the **absence** of an edge asserts an independence. Because of those independencies, the joint factorizes into a product of small **factors** (functions of just a few variables each), and the cost collapses from exponential in $n$ to exponential only in the size of the largest factor.

$$
P(X_1, \dots, X_n) = \frac{1}{Z}\prod_{c} \phi_c(X_c)
$$

Here each $\phi_c$ is a factor over a small subset $X_c$ of variables, and $Z$ is a normalizing constant. The whole field is essentially: which graph encodes my independencies, how do I read independencies back off the graph, and how do I compute and learn with the factored form.

::: {.callout-tip}
The rule of thumb that makes PGMs click: **an edge means possible direct dependence; a missing edge is a promise of conditional independence.** The model's power comes from the edges you *leave out*.
:::

## 13.2 — Bayesian networks: directed models and d-separation

A **Bayesian network** (also called a belief network or directed graphical model) is a directed acyclic graph (DAG) in which each node is a variable and each edge points from a "cause" to an "effect." The semantics are clean: each variable is independent of its non-descendants given its **parents** — this is the **local Markov property** — so the joint factorizes as a product of *local conditional distributions*.

$$
P(X_1, \dots, X_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))
$$

The classic toy example: a sprinkler and rain both make grass wet. *Cloudy* influences both *Rain* and *Sprinkler*; *Rain* and *Sprinkler* both influence *WetGrass*.

```{mermaid}
graph TD
  C[Cloudy] --> S[Sprinkler]
  C --> R[Rain]
  S --> W[WetGrass]
  R --> W
```

Instead of one big table you store four small ones. The art of reading independencies off the DAG is **d-separation** ("directed separation"), which decides whether a path between two variables is "blocked" given an observed set. For a DAG the local property above and the global "every d-separation is an independence" property are *equivalent* — so the single factorization formula and the path-blocking rules below are two views of the same set of promises. The whole thing reduces to three local patterns:

- **Chain** $A \to B \to C$: information flows $A$ to $C$, but observing $B$ blocks it ($A \perp C \mid B$).
- **Fork** $A \leftarrow B \to C$: a common cause; observing $B$ blocks it ($A \perp C \mid B$).
- **Collider** $A \to B \leftarrow C$: a common effect; the path is blocked *by default*, but observing $B$ (or any descendant of $B$) *unblocks* it.

That last case is the subtle, important one. *Rain* and *Sprinkler* are marginally independent, but once you see the grass is wet, they become dependent: if you then learn it rained, the sprinkler becomes a less likely explanation. This is **explaining away**, and it is exactly why colliders behave backwards from the other two patterns.

The **Markov blanket** of a node — the minimal set that, once observed, makes the node independent of everything else — is bigger in a directed model than you might guess. In a Bayesian network it is the node's **parents, its children, and its children's other parents (co-parents)**. The co-parents are there precisely because of explaining away: a shared child couples them. (In the undirected models of §13.3 the blanket is simply the immediate neighbors — keep the two definitions distinct.)

::: {.callout-warning}
A common mistake is to read the arrows as literal causation. A Bayesian network only encodes a valid *factorization* of the joint — many different DAGs can represent the same distribution. Giving the arrows a causal interpretation requires extra assumptions (see the causal-inference chapter); the bare network is about independence, not intervention.
:::

## 13.3 — Markov random fields: undirected models

Some dependencies have no natural direction. Adjacent pixels in an image tend to share a label; neighboring atoms in a protein constrain each other. Forcing arrows onto such symmetric relationships is awkward. **Markov random fields** (MRFs, also called Markov networks) drop the direction and use an undirected graph.

Without arrows there is no notion of "parent," so we cannot factor into conditional distributions. Instead we attach a non-negative **potential** function $\psi_c$ to each **clique** $c$ (a fully connected subset of nodes) and multiply:

$$
P(X) = \frac{1}{Z}\prod_{c \in \text{cliques}} \psi_c(X_c), \qquad Z = \sum_{X}\prod_{c}\psi_c(X_c)
$$

A potential is *not* a probability — it is just a "compatibility score" saying how much the model likes a given joint setting of the clique's variables. The price for that flexibility is the **partition function** $Z$: the sum over all configurations needed to normalize. $Z$ is what makes undirected models harder than directed ones, because computing it is itself a hard inference problem.

The independence rule is pleasingly simple — the **Markov blanket** of a variable is just its immediate neighbors, and a variable is conditionally independent of the rest of the graph given them. To test $A \perp B \mid S$, delete the nodes in $S$; if no path remains between $A$ and $B$, they are independent. One caveat worth stating: this "separation implies independence" guarantee (the *global Markov property*) holds for strictly positive distributions — every configuration must have non-zero probability. Where some configurations are outright forbidden, separation in the graph need not give independence, so the clean rule quietly assumes positivity.

| | Bayesian network (directed) | Markov random field (undirected) |
|---|---|---|
| Edges | arrows (asymmetric) | lines (symmetric) |
| Factors | conditional distributions $P(X_i \mid \text{pa})$ | clique potentials $\psi_c$ |
| Normalized? | locally normalized, $Z=1$ | global $Z$ must be computed |
| Independence test | d-separation (colliders) | graph separation (delete & disconnect) |
| Markov blanket | parents + children + co-parents | immediate neighbors |
| Natural fit | causal / generative stories | spatial, relational, symmetric ties |

A common log-linear form writes $\psi_c(X_c) = \exp(\theta_c \cdot f_c(X_c))$, turning the MRF into an exponential-family model; **conditional random fields** (CRFs), widely used for sequence labeling, are simply MRFs conditioned on inputs.

## 13.4 — Factor graphs and plate notation

Both directed and undirected models ultimately say "the joint is a product of factors." A **factor graph** makes that explicit by drawing two kinds of nodes: round **variable nodes** and square **factor nodes**, with an edge wherever a factor depends on a variable. It is a bipartite graph — variables connect only to factors and vice versa.

```{mermaid}
graph LR
  X1((X1)) --- fa[f_a]
  X2((X2)) --- fa
  X2 --- fb[f_b]
  X3((X3)) --- fb
```

Why bother with a third notation? Because it is unambiguous. A single undirected triangle could mean one potential over three variables or three pairwise potentials — the factor graph distinguishes them by drawing the factor nodes directly. More importantly, the message-passing inference algorithms of §13.6 are cleanest when stated on factor graphs: messages simply flow back and forth along edges between variables and factors. Factor graphs are the lingua franca that lets one inference engine serve Bayesian networks, MRFs, and everything in between.

The other notation every reader meets is **plate notation**, a shorthand for *repeated* structure. When the same sub-graph repeats — one copy per data point, per word, per cluster — drawing all $N$ copies is wasteful. Instead you draw one copy and enclose it in a rectangle (a "plate") labelled with the repeat count $N$; everything inside is replicated, and an arrow crossing into the plate from outside is a parameter shared across all copies. The diagram below shows a global parameter $\theta$ generating $N$ i.i.d. observations $x_i$ — the canonical "$\theta \to x_i$, repeated $N$ times" picture behind mixtures, naive Bayes, and topic models like LDA.

```{mermaid}
graph TD
  theta(("θ")) --> xi(("x_i"))
  subgraph plate["N observations"]
    xi
  end
```

## 13.5 — Hidden Markov Models: the workhorse sequence model

The **Hidden Markov Model** (HMM) is the most-used PGM in practice and the easiest to picture. You have a sequence of hidden **states** $z_1, z_2, \dots, z_T$ that evolve over time, and at each step you only observe an **emission** $x_t$ generated by the current state. Think of a person walking through rooms (hidden) while you only hear the sounds from each room (observed).

Two assumptions make it tractable. The **Markov property**: the next state depends only on the current state, not the whole history. And the **emission independence**: each observation depends only on the current state. This gives a tidy factorization:

$$
P(z_{1:T}, x_{1:T}) = P(z_1)\prod_{t=2}^{T} P(z_t \mid z_{t-1}) \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(x_t \mid z_t)
$$

```{mermaid}
graph LR
  z1((z1)) --> z2((z2)) --> z3((z3))
  z1 --> x1((x1))
  z2 --> x2((x2))
  z3 --> x3((x3))
```

An HMM is defined by three things: an initial distribution $\pi$, a **transition matrix** $A$ where $A_{ij} = P(z_t = j \mid z_{t-1} = i)$, and **emission** probabilities $B$ where $B_{jk} = P(x_t = k \mid z_t = j)$.

Three questions come up, each with its own classic algorithm:

- **Likelihood** — how probable is an observed sequence? Solved by the **forward** algorithm.
- **Decoding** — what is the single most likely hidden path? Solved by **Viterbi**.
- **Learning** — fit $\pi, A, B$ from observations only? Solved by **Baum–Welch** (an EM algorithm built on forward–backward).

The **forward–backward** algorithm computes, for every time step, the posterior over states given the *whole* sequence, by sweeping a "forward" message left-to-right and a "backward" message right-to-left and combining them. **Viterbi** has the same shape but replaces the sum with a max and remembers the argmax, recovering the best path by backtracking. Both are dynamic programming, $O(T K^2)$ for $K$ states — linear in sequence length, which is why HMMs scaled to real speech recognition, part-of-speech tagging, and gene-finding in bioinformatics long before deep learning.

Here is Viterbi from scratch, which makes the recursion concrete. Notice the self-check is deliberately *adversarial*: the very first observation points to the wrong state in isolation, so a decoder that trusted the prior and emission alone would start in state 0 — only the sticky transitions drag the path onto the right track, which is exactly the dynamic program we want to exercise.

```python
import numpy as np

def viterbi(obs, pi, A, B):
    # pi: init [K], A: transition [K,K], B: emission [K,n_obs]
    T, K = len(obs), len(pi)
    delta = np.zeros((T, K))       # best score of a path ending in state k at t
    psi   = np.zeros((T, K), int)  # backpointer: best previous state
    delta[0] = pi * B[:, obs[0]]
    for t in range(1, T):
        for k in range(K):
            scores = delta[t-1] * A[:, k]   # come from any prev state
            psi[t, k] = scores.argmax()      # remember the best one
            delta[t, k] = scores.max() * B[k, obs[t]]
    # backtrack from the best final state
    path = [int(delta[-1].argmax())]
    for t in range(T-1, 0, -1):
        path.append(int(psi[t, path[-1]]))
    return path[::-1]

# adversarial self-check: obs[0]=1 alone screams "state 1" (prior favours 0
# only weakly, emission of symbol 1 favours state 1), yet the true path stays
# in state 0 because transitions are highly sticky and the rest of the
# sequence is state-0 evidence. Only the DP gets this right.
pi = np.array([0.5, 0.5])
A  = np.array([[0.99, 0.01], [0.5, 0.5]])   # state 0 is very sticky
B  = np.array([[0.4, 0.6],               # state 0 actually emits symbol 1 a bit more
               [0.5, 0.5]])              # state 1 is uninformative
# whole sequence is symbol 1; sticky state 0 should win the entire path
assert viterbi([1, 1, 1, 1], pi, A, B) == [0, 0, 0, 0]
```

A practical wrinkle the toy code hides: multiplying many probabilities together drives `delta` toward zero and **underflows** to $0.0$ on a real-length sequence. The standard fixes are to work in **log-space** (turn the products into sums of $\log$ and the `max` stays a `max`) for Viterbi, and to **rescale** the forward messages at each step (or use the log-sum-exp trick) for forward–backward, where the sum cannot be done in log-space term by term. Always assume you will need one of these before an HMM touches real data.

For completeness, here is the forward recursion — the *sum* counterpart of Viterbi, and the engine inside Baum–Welch — with the per-step rescaling that keeps it numerically alive:

```python
def forward_loglik(obs, pi, A, B):
    # returns log P(obs) using per-step rescaling to avoid underflow
    T, K = len(obs), len(pi)
    alpha = pi * B[:, obs[0]]          # unnormalised forward message
    c = alpha.sum(); alpha /= c        # rescale, remember the scale
    loglik = np.log(c)
    for t in range(1, T):
        alpha = (alpha @ A) * B[:, obs[t]]   # propagate then re-weight
        c = alpha.sum(); alpha /= c          # rescale again
        loglik += np.log(c)                  # log P(obs) = sum of log scales
    return loglik
```

## 13.6 — Inference: exact and approximate

**Inference** means answering a query — usually a marginal $P(X_i \mid \text{evidence})$ or the most probable configuration — from the factored joint. Everything else (decisions, learning, predictions) rides on top of it.

The basic exact method is **variable elimination**: to marginalize out a variable, gather every factor that mentions it, multiply them, and sum the variable away, producing a new factor. Repeat until only the query variables remain. It is correct and simple, but its cost is exponential in the **treewidth** — informally, the size of the largest intermediate factor you are forced to create. The order in which you eliminate matters enormously, and finding the best order is itself NP-hard.

**Belief propagation** (the sum–product algorithm) reorganizes elimination as *message passing* on a factor graph: each node sends its neighbors a summary of everything it knows, and after messages settle, every variable's marginal is read off locally. Stated precisely, there are two message types. A **variable-to-factor** message is the product of everything that variable hears from its *other* factors; a **factor-to-variable** message multiplies in the factor itself and sums out the factor's other variables:

$$
\mu_{x \to f}(x) = \prod_{g \in N(x)\setminus\{f\}} \mu_{g \to x}(x)
$$

$$
\mu_{f \to x}(x) = \sum_{X_f \setminus \{x\}} f(X_f)\!\!\prod_{y \in N(f)\setminus\{x\}} \mu_{y \to f}(y)
$$

Here $N(x)$ are the factors touching variable $x$, $N(f)$ the variables touching factor $f$, and $X_f$ the variables in factor $f$. A variable's marginal is then the product of all incoming factor messages, $P(x) \propto \prod_{f \in N(x)} \mu_{f \to x}(x)$. The "max-product" / Viterbi variant just swaps the $\sum$ for a $\max$. On a tree the messages converge to the exact answer in two sweeps. On a graph with loops, running the same updates anyway gives **loopy belief propagation** — no guarantee of correctness, but often a strong approximation.

```{mermaid}
graph TD
  E[Inference query] --> X{Graph has loops?}
  X -->|tree / low treewidth| EX[Exact: variable elimination / belief propagation]
  X -->|high treewidth| AP[Approximate]
  AP --> S[Sampling: MCMC, Gibbs]
  AP --> V[Variational: optimize a simpler q]
```

When exact inference is hopeless, two families of approximation dominate:

- **Sampling (Monte Carlo).** Draw samples from the distribution and estimate quantities by counting. **Gibbs sampling** is the natural fit here: resample each variable from its conditional given its Markov blanket, cycle through all variables, and the samples eventually look like draws from the true joint. (Recall the blanket differs by model — neighbors in an MRF, but parents + children + co-parents in a directed network from §13.2; use the right one or the conditional is wrong.) Simple and asymptotically exact, but can be slow to mix.
- **Variational inference.** Pick a tractable family of distributions $q$ and find the member closest to the true posterior $p$ by minimizing $\mathrm{KL}(q \,\|\, p)$. This turns inference into optimization — fast and scalable, but it gives a *biased* approximation and tends to underestimate uncertainty.

::: {.callout-warning}
Exact inference is only "easy" on trees and low-treewidth graphs. In general it is NP-hard, and so is computing the partition function $Z$. Adding a few edges to a sparse model can silently make exact inference intractable — always check the structure before assuming you can compute marginals exactly.
:::

## 13.7 — Learning parameters and structure

So far the graph and its numbers were given. **Learning** asks where they come from. There are two distinct problems.

**Parameter learning** fixes the graph and fits the factors. If all variables are observed, a directed model is wonderfully easy: each local conditional is fit independently, and maximum likelihood is just counting (with smoothing) — count how often each child value co-occurs with each parent configuration. Undirected models are harder even when fully observed, because the shared $Z$ couples all parameters; you typically optimize the (regularized) log-likelihood by gradient methods, paying for inference at each step.

When some variables are **latent** (never observed — the hidden states of an HMM, cluster identities, missing labels), you cannot just count. The standard tool is **Expectation–Maximization (EM)**: alternate between an **E-step** that uses current parameters to infer a soft distribution over the hidden variables, and an **M-step** that maximizes the *expected* complete-data log-likelihood under that distribution — that is, it updates parameters using **expected counts** (the soft posterior weights), not a single best guess imputed as if it were observed. (Imputing the single most likely hidden value and counting is "hard EM," a cruder approximation — true EM weights every possibility by its posterior probability.) Baum–Welch for HMMs is exactly EM with forward–backward as its E-step. EM climbs the likelihood monotonically but only to a local optimum, so initialization matters.

**Structure learning** is the harder, less-settled problem: discovering the *edges* themselves from data. Two broad strategies exist. *Score-based* methods search over candidate graphs, scoring each by a penalized likelihood (e.g. BIC, which rewards fit and punishes extra edges) — but the space of DAGs is super-exponential, so the search is heuristic. *Constraint-based* methods run statistical conditional-independence tests and keep only edges consistent with the results. Both are sensitive to limited data, and for directed models the recovered structure is generally only identifiable up to an equivalence class of graphs that encode the same independencies.

## 13.8 — Relation to modern deep generative models

It is tempting to think PGMs were replaced by deep learning. The truth is they were absorbed. Many deep generative models *are* graphical models whose factors happen to be neural networks.

A **variational autoencoder** is the clearest case: it is a tiny Bayesian network, latent $z \to$ observation $x$, where the decoder $P(x \mid z)$ is a neural net and the encoder is a variational $q(z \mid x)$ trained by exactly the variational inference of §13.6 — its loss, the ELBO, is the same $\mathrm{KL}$ objective. **Diffusion models** unroll into a long Markov chain of latent variables, a deep cousin of the HMM's chain structure. Even the attention pattern of a transformer can be read as a learned, dynamic dependency graph over tokens.

What deep learning added was *expressive factors and amortized inference*: rather than a small probability table per factor, a neural network parameterizes the conditional, and rather than re-running inference per example, a network learns to predict the posterior directly. The graphical-model layer still supplies the scaffolding — what depends on what, what is latent, how the joint factorizes, and what objective inference optimizes. Knowing PGMs is what lets you see the probabilistic skeleton inside an otherwise opaque deep model.

::: {.callout-tip}
When you meet a new generative model, ask the three PGM questions: *What are the variables, which are latent, and how does the joint factorize?* The answer usually reveals the model to be a familiar graphical structure with neural-net factors bolted on.
:::

## 13.9 — Key takeaways

- A graph encodes conditional independencies, which let a joint distribution factorize into small local factors — escaping the exponential blow-up of a full joint table.
- **Bayesian networks** are directed (factors are conditional distributions, independence read by d-separation); the local and global Markov properties are equivalent for a DAG, and the collider/"explaining away" pattern is the tricky one.
- **Markov random fields** are undirected (factors are clique potentials, independence by graph separation, valid for strictly positive distributions); the global partition function $Z$ is what makes them costly.
- The **Markov blanket** differs by model: neighbors in an MRF, but parents + children + co-parents in a Bayesian network — this matters for Gibbs sampling.
- **Factor graphs** are the unifying bipartite notation and the natural home for message-passing; **plate notation** compresses repeated i.i.d. structure into a single boxed copy.
- **HMMs** model sequences via hidden states and emissions; forward, Viterbi, and Baum–Welch are linear-time dynamic programming over the chain — use log-space or rescaling to dodge numerical underflow.
- **Belief propagation** is the central inference idea: variable→factor and factor→variable messages, exact on trees, approximate (loopy) on general graphs; otherwise fall back to **Gibbs sampling** or **variational** methods.
- **Learning** splits into parameter learning (counting, gradients, or EM — which maximizes *expected* complete-data log-likelihood — for latents) and the harder structure learning (score- or constraint-based search).
- Modern deep generative models (VAEs, diffusion) are graphical models with neural-network factors; the PGM lens reveals their probabilistic skeleton.

## 13.10 — See also

- **Chapter 12 — Bayesian Machine Learning**: priors, posteriors, and the inference machinery PGMs build on.
- **Chapter 17 — Recurrent & Sequence Models**: the neural successors to the HMM for sequence modeling.
- **Chapter 19 — Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning** and **Chapter 20 — Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows**: where variational inference meets deep nets.
- **Chapter 21 — Diffusion Models**: deep Markov chains of latent variables.
- **Chapter 28 — Natural Language Processing** and **Chapter 29 — Speech & Audio Processing**: classic application grounds for HMMs and CRFs.
- **Chapter 45 — Causal Inference & Causal ML**: when directed edges may be read as causes, not just factorizations.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 12 · 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning](ch12.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [14 · 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals →](ch14.html)
